content.full_text:
BELL & HILTACHK

Attorneys and Counselors at Law

555 Capitol Mall
Suite 530
Sacramento CA 95814

Charles H. Bell, Jr.                          Telephone: (916) 442-7757
Thomas W. Hiltachk                            Facsimile: (916) 442-7759


                         September 30, 1992




Mr. Wayne Imberi
Political Reform Consultant
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 700
Sacramento, CA  95814

     Re:  Confirmation of Telephone Advice

Dear Mr. Imberi:

     This letter is to confirm telephone advice given to
Chris Micheli of our office on September 29, 1992.

                            First Issue

     Factual Background: Government Code section 84203.5(a)
requires that a report be filed if independent expenditures of
$500 or more are made during a calendar year.  FPPC Manual E
(page 9) and Form 461 specify that an independent expenditure
filing is to be made only if independent expenditures of $1,000
or more are made in a calendar year.

     Question 1: Why is there an inconsistency between the
statute and the manual, as well as the instructions for filing an
independent expenditure report (Form 461)?

     Commission Advice: If independent expenditures of
$1,000 or more are made in a calendar year, then the person would
qualify as a "committee" under section 82013(b).  Only then would
section 84203.5 come into play.  Form 465 is an "outgrowth" of
section 84203.5 and is the report form to be used in only certain
circumstances.  If, on the other hand, less than $1,000 in
independent expenditures are made, then section 84203.5 is not
applicable because the person is not a "committee."

     Therefore, if a person makes $1,000 or more in
independent expenditures during a calendar year, he or she must
file a Form 461.  In addition, if a person qualifies as a
committee and expands over $500 in independent expenditures in a
calendar year for a single candidate or a single ballot measure,

Mr. Wayne Imberi
September 30, 1992
Page 2

then the person must file Form 461 and Form 465.

Question 2: What are the filing obligations of an
individual who makes $750 in independent expenditures and how are
they reported?

Commission Advice: If independent expenditures in the
total amount of $750 (i.e., less than $1,000) are made in a
calendar year, there are no filing obligations. This is due to
the fact that the person has not qualified as a "committee" under
section 82013(b). Hence, there is no obligation to file a Form
461.

In addition, if independent expenditures of $750 are
made in support of or in opposition to a single candidate or
ballot measure, then there are no filing obligations under
section 84203.5. Hence, no Form 461 and no Form 465 need to be
filed.

Second Issue

Factual Background: A city has a local campaign
finance ordinance which imposes different filing obligations than
those found in the Political Reform Act.

Question 3: What are the obligations of a committee
with regard to the additional requirements of the local
ordinance? Must a committee comply with both the Act and the
local ordinance, or should it comply with only the Act's filing
provisions?

Commission Advice: Pursuant to the local campaign
ordinance, the filer must complete and file any pre-election
campaign reports specified by the ordinance. Government Code
section 81009.5 was amended to clarify that local jurisdictions
may impose additional filing requirements. In other words, a
committee must comply with both the Act and the local ordinance
where their provisions do not conflict. Hence, if the ordinance
requires additional filings be made during the pre-election
period, then those filings must be done by the committee.

Question 4: Does a state major donor have to comply
with the requirements of the local campaign ordinance when he or
she complies with the Act's filing obligations?

Commission Advice: Where the state major donor makes
contributions to state and local candidates and/or ballot
measures in excess of $10,000, he or she must file a major donor
report with the Secretary of State, County of Los Angeles and

Mr. Wayne Imberi
September 30, 1992
Page 3


County of San Francisco. Form 461 is to be used by the filer.

However, if a local ordinance imposes additional filing
obligations (e.g., for those who contribute to a local ballot
measure committee), then the state major donor must comply with
those additional requirements found in the ordinance. This is
due to the fact that a city, pursuant to Government Code section
81009.5, may impose additional filing obligations on a committee,
so long as they do not conflict with the Act's provisions.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter. If
I can provide any additional information, please feel free to
contact me.

Very truly yours,

Charles H. Bell, Jr.

CHB/man
1106.01

FPPC

GET | 2 54 PM '92

sections.question:
Why is there an inconsistency between the statute and the manual, as well as the instructions for filing an independent expenditure report (Form 461)?

Commission Advice: If independent expenditures of $1,000 or more are made in a calendar year, then the person would qualify as a "committee" under section 82013(b). Only then would section 84203.5 come into play. Form 465 is an "outgrowth" of section 84203.5 and is the report form to be used in only certain circumstances. If, on the other hand, less than $1,000 in independent expenditures are made, then section 84203.5 is not applicable because the person is not a "committee."

Therefore, if a person makes $1,000 or more in independent expenditures during a calendar year, he or she must file a Form 461. In addition, if a person qualifies as a committee and expends over $500 in independent expenditures in a calendar year for a single candidate or a single ballot measure,

then the person must file Form 461 and Form 465.

Question 2: What are the filing obligations of an individual who makes $750 in independent expenditures and how are they reported?

Commission Advice: If independent expenditures in the total amount of $750 (i.e., less than $1,000) are made in a calendar year, there are no filing obligations. This is due to the fact that the person has not qualified as a "committee" under section 82013(b). Hence, there is no obligation to file a Form 461.

In addition, if independent expenditures of $750 are made in support of or in opposition to a single candidate or ballot measure, then there are no filing obligations under section 84203.5. Hence, no Form 461 and no Form 465 need to be filed.

Second Issue

Factual Background: A city has a local campaign finance ordinance which imposes different filing obligations than those found in the Political Reform Act.

Question 3: What are the obligations of a committee with regard to the additional requirements of the local ordinance? Must a committee comply with both the Act and the local ordinance, or should it comply with only the Act's filing provisions?

Commission Advice: Pursuant to the local campaign ordinance, the filer must complete and file any pre-election campaign reports specified by the ordinance. Government Code section 81009.5 was amended to clarify that local jurisdictions may impose additional filing requirements. In other words, a committee must comply with both the Act and the local ordinance where their provisions do not conflict. Hence, if the ordinance requires additional filings be made during the pre-election period, then those filings must be done by the committee.

Question 4: Does a state major donor have to comply with the requirements of the local campaign ordinance when he or she complies with the Act's filing obligations?

Commission Advice: Where the state major donor makes contributions to state and local candidates and/or ballot measures in excess of $10,000, he or she must file a major donor report with the Secretary of State, County of Los Angeles and

Mr. Wayne Imberi  
September 30, 1992  

County of San Francisco. Form 461 is to be used by the filer.

However, if a local ordinance imposes additional filing obligations (e.g., for those who contribute to a local ballot measure committee), then the state major donor must comply with those additional requirements found in the ordinance. This is due to the fact that a city, pursuant to Government Code section 81009.5, may impose additional filing obligations on a committee, so long as they do not conflict with the Act's provisions.

Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter. If I can provide any additional information, please feel free to contact me.
